to be greatly concerned over the
problem Of supplying work and pay
for idle transients now drifting into
Spokane as they drift every win
ter into convenient cities.


A published report Of the mayors
convictions in the matter makes
It appear that Spokane is particu-
larIy afflicted with the idle and
apt to be hungry, and that there is
something disturbingly unusual
and ominous In the influx from out
side points this winter. The inn
preston an outsider gains from
this 10 likely to be neither favora-
Bile nor fair to spokane's actual
condition.


Men Of family and means are
not, as a rule, consumed with
anxiety to entrust them to a city,
the streets of which are throneed
with starving men, desperate for
something to do at any price that
will procure food and bed.


Yet this Is the picture drawn of
Spokane in TIE announcement Of
the mayors plans to provide for
transient labor. It presents a
gloomy contrast with the boasted
industrial resources of the Inland
Empire and unfortunately its truth
fuIness is appreciable only to those
who are conversant with actual To-
cal conditions.


However, there is much worthy
work to be done In providing en-
pIoyment for one particular class
Of labor-the resident class. Its
interests, although Of vital import
ance to every branch of civic &C.
tivity, seem to have been sinsularIy
overlooked in this outburst Of
phiIanthropic enterprise. The fact
that bed and board could be OBJ
tanned In Spokane all winter
through public relief work, which
amounts to about the same thing as
public relief play, would no doubt
be cheerful information for the
man satisfied with the bed and
board to be obtained by public re-
lief. In all probability such Of him
as there are would benefit the
cheap lodging houses and restaur.
ants to the extent Of several
months patronage in consequence.


Meanwhile the resident laboring
man, the man with taxes to pay
out of the earnings of his hands,
a home to maintain, children to
feed and clothe and keep in school
The must get through the winter
the best he can. The city ordi-
nance providing a wage scale that
means ivins-and little more
for the family whose bread winner
has no means Of procuring the
necessities of life other than by
welding heavy tools-is already
openly violated without an effort
on the part of civic authorities to
enforce Il. The violation means
nothing to the taxpayer, as the con-
tractor estimates on the legal rate
for labor and profits by what he
saves on cheap workmen.


For man with family, eompeti-
ton with floating labor under these
conditions would be hard enough,
but the mayors scheme is designed
to create species Of competition
at the rate Of rs cents or 81 a day.
Relief work Is intended, but the
competition Is created because the
